title: The Classical CV Pipeline
concept: Classical computer vision chains hand-designed filters — blur, sharpen, edge detection, Sobel, Canny — into a fixed, ordered pipeline that an engineer builds step by step to pull useful features out of a raw image.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does a Sobel filter actually measure in an image?

options.text: How sharply brightness changes between neighboring pixels
How large the image file is in kilobytes

exercises.explain: Right — Sobel measures brightness gradients. Today we look at how filters like this get chained together into a full pipeline.

cards.title: An assembly line for pixels
cards.body: Imagine a photo moving down a factory conveyor belt — one station blurs it, the next sharpens it, the next traces its outlines. That's exactly how classical computer vision engineers built feature extraction: by chaining fixed filters into one ordered pipeline.

cards.title: What is a classical CV pipeline?
cards.body: A classical CV pipeline is a fixed, ordered sequence of hand-designed filters — for example blur, then sharpen, then Sobel, then Canny — where each step's output becomes the next step's input, all wired together by a human engineer.

cards.title: A recipe with fixed steps
cards.body: Think of a chef following a strict recipe: wash, chop, sauté, season, plate — always in that order. A CV pipeline works the same way: clean up noise first, enhance next, then extract edges last — a fixed recipe an engineer wrote once and reuses on every image.

cards.title: Reading a license plate
cards.body: A license-plate reader might first blur a photo slightly to kill sensor noise, then run Sobel to find where brightness jumps sharply (the edges of each character), then run Canny on top to get a clean, thin outline of every digit — three filters chained into one pipeline.

cards.title: The pipeline doesn't 'understand' anything
cards.body: It's tempting to think a filter pipeline knows it's looking at a license plate. It doesn't — every step runs the exact same fixed math on every pixel of every image, whether that image shows a plate, a face, or a cloud.

cards.title: Single filter vs. pipeline
cards.body: One operation vs. a chained sequence:

left.text:
One fixed operation
e.g. just Sobel alone

right.text:
Multiple filters chained
Blur → Sharpen → Sobel → Canny

exercises.prompt: Which best describes a classical CV pipeline?

options.text: A fixed sequence of hand-designed filters, where each step's output feeds the next
A single filter that runs twice on the same image
A neural network that updates its own weights during use
A random shuffle of unrelated image filters

exercises.explain: A pipeline is an ordered, human-designed chain — each filter's output becomes the next filter's input.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In a classical CV pipeline, an engineer decides the order and design of every filter step by hand.
exercises.explain: True. Every step and its order is chosen by a human engineer, not learned automatically.

exercises.sentence: In a classical pipeline, the ___ of one filter becomes the input to the next filter in the chain.
exercises.explain: Output is correct — each filter passes its result forward to the next stage of the pipeline.

exercises.prompt: Which sequence best describes a realistic classical CV pipeline for finding clean object outlines?

options.text: Blur to reduce noise, then Sobel to find gradients, then Canny to thin and threshold the result
Resize the image, then compress it to JPEG, then rename the file
Sharpen, then convert to grayscale, then increase the file size

exercises.explain: That order — blur, then Sobel, then Canny — is exactly the kind of layered pipeline classical CV engineers built by hand.

exercises.prompt: You're building a pipeline to pull clean edges out of noisy security-camera footage. Which order makes the most sense, and why?

options.text: Blur first to suppress sensor noise, then Sobel to find gradients, then Canny to thin and threshold — each step cleans up for the next
Run Canny first and blur afterward, since order never matters in a pipeline
Sharpen first to amplify every pixel, including the noise, before looking for edges
Skip filters entirely and just count how many colors appear in the frame

exercises.explain: Exactly — order matters because each filter's fixed math assumes a cleaner input than the last. But hand-building the right pipeline for every scenario is exactly the problem we dig into next: hand-crafted filters don't scale forever.
